ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a whole wheat bread entry; the butter and sugar toppings are not separately coded — closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and similar products — wholemeal/wholegrain bread
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers wholemeal bread as a category; butter and sugar toppings are not separately faceted in this code — closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT code found for wholemeal bread with butter and sugar as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is two slices of wholemeal bread (approximately 70 g) spread with butter and sprinkled with sugar, commonly eaten as a breakfast or snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal Bread with Butter and Sugar — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Wholemeal Bread with Butter and Sugar (cr_id F05020133). All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100g based on typical butter contribution; this field is not separately reported in the HPB record and is documented as an estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wholemeal / Whole Wheat Bread — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 69 (medium) is consistent with published University of Sydney GI Database values for wholemeal bread (GI range 60–74 depending on formulation). The composite dish (wholemeal bread + butter + sugar) does not have a direct GI entry; the GI is attributed primarily to the wholemeal bread component, as fat and sugar additions modestly affect the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 44.29 × 0.70) / 100 ≈ 21.4 for a 70 g serving. Input record GL of 30.6 appears to have been calculated on a per-100g basis (69 × 44.29 / 100 ≈ 30.6), which is retained in notes for reference.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (17.66 g/100g), elevated sodium (243 mg/100g), saturated fat from butter (~7.5 g/100g), and very low potassium (4 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this food due to high added sugar exceeding 10 g/100g for a solid food, elevated sodium, saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness, and near-zero potassium; replace butter with avocado or unsalted nut butter and eliminate added sugar entirely.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar at 17.66 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g threshold; medium GI of 69 combined with high sugar load raises postprandial glucose risk; saturated fat from butter worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this food as the high added sugar content (17.66 g/100g) and medium GI (69) create a significant postprandial glucose spike; if wholemeal bread is desired, consume plain without added sugar and replace butter with a small amount of unsaturated fat spread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 7.5 g/100g from butter, cholesterol 37.06 mg/100g, high added sugar 17.66 g/100g, and sodium 243 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this food due to the combination of high saturated fat from butter, elevated cholesterol, high added sugar promoting dyslipidaemia and inflammation, and moderate sodium; substitute with plain wholemeal bread with extra-virgin olive oil or avocado spread and no added sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; sugar at 17.66 g/100g exceeds the 15 g/100g pro-inflammatory threshold; potential trans fat from butter
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food as the high saturated fat from butter and high added sugar are both strongly pro-inflammatory; replace with wholemeal bread with extra-virgin olive oil and no added sugar.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal Bread with Butter and Sugar is a simple bread-based dish consisting of sliced wholemeal (whole wheat) bread spread with butter and sprinkled with white sugar. It is a common breakfast or snack item in Singapore and Malaysia, reflecting British colonial culinary influence on local eating habits. The wholemeal bread provides a dense, slightly nutty flavour and chewy texture from the whole grain content, while the butter adds richness and fat, and the sugar provides sweetness. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense at 339 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (8.4 g), significant total fat (15.03 g, predominantly saturated from butter at approximately 7.5 g/100g), and high carbohydrate content (44.29 g) including a high added sugar load of 17.66 g per 100 g. Dietary fibre is moderate at 3.81 g/100g from the wholemeal flour. Sodium is elevated at 243 mg/100g from both the bread and butter. Potassium is very low at 4 mg/100g. The glycemic index is medium (GI 69), but the high sugar content significantly raises the glycemic load. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high added sugar, saturated fat, and sodium content. The wholemeal base provides some nutritional benefit through fibre and iron (3.91 mg/100g) and calcium (124 mg/100g), but these are outweighed by the adverse nutritional profile of the butter and sugar additions. For health-conscious consumers, plain wholemeal bread without added sugar or butter is a significantly better option.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020133), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. The record is marked as canonical with confidence 0.95 in the input. Saturated fat is estimated from typical butter composition and documented accordingly. GI is based on analogous wholemeal bread data from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) is estimated based on typical butter fatty acid composition (approximately 50% saturated fat) applied to the total fat content attributable to butter in this dish; this value is not separately reported in the HPB source. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this composite dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database as of 2026. Vitamin and mineral micronutrients beyond potassium, calcium, iron, and cholesterol are set to -1 as they are not reported in the HPB source for this entry. 3. Glycemic load per serving (21.4) is calculated for a 70 g serving: GL = (69 × 44.29 × 0.70) / 100 ≈ 21.4. The input record GL of 30.6 was calculated on a per-100g basis (69 × 44.29 / 100 ≈ 30.6) and is retained here for reference. 4. The input health_suitability referenced trans fat of 0.1 g/100g for the rheumatoid arthritis assessment; this value is not confirmed in the HPB source and trans_fat_g is set to -1 pending verification. 5. Potassium at 4 mg/100g is unusually low and may reflect the HPB source value for this specific composite preparation; this value is preserved as reported. 6. This food is classified as processed (not ultra-processed) as it is a home-style preparation of bread with butter and sugar rather than an industrially formulated product.
